course_title: Writing and Publishing for Children and Young Adults
course_description:
This course considers the vibrant segment of the trade publishing market that is children's and young adult writing, both from the perspective of the publishing professional and the creative writer. While the conditions of publishing have seen considerable disruptions and reconfigurations since the turn of the 21st century, the children's and young adult market has remained reliably significant in both economic and artistic terms. The course will consider the ways in which writing for children and young adults may differ from writing for other audiences; the particular ways in which these texts are produced, marketed, and circulated; and how authors and publishers relate to younger readers.

This course examines the position of Children's and Young Adult literature in the contemporary publishing industry, from both a writer's and industry professional's perspective. The course includes both a creative writing and a practical writing component.

Additional timetable information
Whilst every effort is made to place students in their preferred activity, it is not always possible for a student to be enrolled in their tutorial of choice. If you require assistance, please ensure that you email

from your
UQ student email
with:
Your name
Your student ID
The course code
A list of three tutorial preferences (in order of preference)
Reason for the change – e.g. timetable clash, elite athlete status, SAP
Teaching staff do not have access to change tutorials or help with timetables; all timetabling changes must be processed through the Timetabling Team.
course_aims: This course will help students develop a better understanding of the position of Children's and Young Adult literature in the contemporary Australian publishing market through a series of lectures and practical writing-based tutorials and discussions.

learning_outcomes.description: demonstrate an understanding of the position that Children's and Young Adult literature holds in Australian publishing
understand the creative and ethical issues specific to writing, editing, and publishing for children and young adults
write effectively for middle grade and/or young adult audiences.

assessment_details.title: Reading Exercises
assessment_details.due_date: After January: 1/08/2025 4:00 pm Wonder 22/08/2025 4:00 pm The Hate U Give 5/09/2025 4:00 pm Danny the Champion of the World: 26/09/2025 4:00 pm
assessment_details.task_description: There will be 4 multi-choice exercises based on the set readings, run through Blackboard. Each exercise is worth 5 marks, for a total of 20 marks. Students are asked not to collaborate on the exercises and to submit their own work. All of the exercises will be available to complete from Week 1, but must be completed by the set deadline. This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. assessment_details.title: Teachers' Notes
assessment_details.other_conditions: Time limited. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: During the Week 7 seminar, the following in-person exam will be conducted using strict examination procedures. As this is an internal course, a student must make themself available to undertake their examination at the scheduled date, time, and venue. Please see Exam Procedures for full information. Mobile phones, ipads, apple watches and other electronic devices must be turned off or in airplane mode and placed on the floor under a student's desk. Where a devise emits any noise, the student may be subject to allegations of misconduct and the items may be confiscated for the duration of the examination. The exam - Teachers Notes Working from Shauna's Great Expectations by Kathleen Loughnan, write a section or sections that might be included in a set of teachers' notes. You must include at least one writing exercise based on the novel for students to attempt. The audience for the notes is students (not teachers, although teachers might use the notes to help with learning outcomes in the classroom). Aim to produce material that shows deep insight into this novel, and then provokes discussion and debate among students about the themes and characterisations that the author has used throughout the work. Exemplar Teachers' Notes written by industry professionals are provided to help you plan (in advance) for this in-class exam. Format 90 minute written exam. Teacher's Notes should be handwritten during the Week 7 Seminar. This is an in-person test. Wordcount: 800 - 1000 words (about 3 -7 handwritten pages). You may use dot points where appropriate for lists. This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.exam_details: Planning time no planning time minutes Duration 90 minutes Calculator options Any calculator permitted Open/closed book Closed book examination - no written materials permitted Materials You will be permitted to bring suitable writing implements (in a clear container), water in a clear bottle with the label removed, disposable ear plugs. Exam platform Paper based Invigilation Invigilated in person
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Submit written exam paper directly to your instructor at the end of the exam.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to defer this exam .
Note the Delayed Examinations criteria only apply if you have a clash with another examination scheduled for the same session.
Deferred Examination (please also read ECP for full details). There is no alternate assessment available for this examination. Instead, a deferred exam will be organised soon after the original exam under the same conditions.
Acceptable reasons for missing the original exam are detailed in the policies and procedures tab on the ECP. They do not include 'the bus was late' or 'I took a last minute shift at work'.
assessment_details.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam .

assessment_details.title: Writing Portfolio
assessment_details.other_conditions: Peer assessment factor. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: Your writing portfolio will include a query letter to a publisher incorporating a synopsis and a 2200-2400 word writing sample. There is a pass/fail viva for this assignment. The Query Letter (600 words max total) The letter should include a compelling intro which includes the novel's word count, comparative titles and genre, an appropriate synopsis (about 300 words), a brief author bio, and a professional closing. This submission is the result of workshopping with your peers in-person/remotely. The Writing Sample (2200-2400 range) Include the opening of a Middle Grade or Young Adult novel, in the genre of the student's choice. This submission is the result of workshopping with your peers in seminars/remotely. Total combined PORTFOLIO wordcount: approx 3000 words. Do not go under 2700 words or over 3000 words. If a submission exceeds the specified word range, the content of the submission must be assessed and the element of non-compliance marked through a separate criterion, and/or the assessment marked up to the specified word limit. Format 12pt Times New Roman, double-spaced, first line of each section set left, subsequent paragraph first lines are inset. Student name, student number and page number in the footer. Wordcounts, title of novel, AI statement on a front page. This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Written assignments will be submitted electronically via Blackboard and using TurnItIn . Before submitting any assignments for this course you must ensure you have completed UQ's compulsory online Academic Integrity Tutorial.
When you successfully submit your assessment to TurnItIn you will see text confirming your submission is complete, before being redirected to your Assignment inbox. On this page you can:
View the name of the submitted file View date and time of the upload Resubmit your paper (if necessary) Download your submitted paper Download digital receipt.
If you cannot see your submission in your Assignment inbox you should regard your submission as unsuccessful. Students are responsible for retaining evidence of submission by the due date for all assessment items, in the required form (e.g. screenshot, email, photo, and an unaltered copy of submitted work).
If the submission was not successful:
Note the error message (preferably take a screenshot). Go to your assignment page and see if it is possible to submit again. If you cannot submit again email your course coordinator immediately with the assignment attached.
Please visit this webpage for further advice on how to submit your TurnItIn assignment .
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
You can apply for an extension for the written portfolio. If you require an extension for the viva, please contact course convener directly.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: Viva
assessment_details.due_date: 27/10/2025 Times for your viva during the Week 13 seminar will be scheduled nearer the due date.
assessment_details.task_description: 5 minute Viva: Your Writing Portfolio is worth 50% of the course. It includes a 5-minute verbal exam (viva) to discuss your work in week 13 which is a pass/fail hurdle. This means that if you are unable to demonstrate your research and writing process, idea formation, challenges faced and addressed during the creation of your final Writing Portfolio, you will fail the Writing Portfolio assessment (50%). All Vivas will be recorded. Recordings will be retained by the School of Communication and Arts for at least 12 months from the release of the final grade for the course. Recordings will be stored in a secure manner and will only be accessed by authorised school staff for the purposes of: Moderation of marking; Provision of feedback to the student(s) recorded; and/or Re-marking following a successful re-mark application. This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
In person oral viva
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
Please contact course coordinator if you cannot attend the viva in Week 13.
Late submission
By appointment.

learning_activities.topic: Week 1: From Morality Tales to Magic - The Birth of Children's & YA Literature An overview of the Children's and Young Adult book category in Australia, its origins and the western literary canon. We'll also be working on a project plan. Learning outcomes: L01, L02

Week 2: Writing Openings That Pass the Library Browse Test - Guest speaker, Author Nick Earls Leading Children's and Young Adult author Nick Earls will give insights to issues specific to the category. Be sure to have read After January. We'll also be working on how to open the first scene of your novel. Learning outcomes: L02, L03
Week 3: Research & Preparation Week NO SEMINAR THIS WEEK due to the Ekka public holiday. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

Week 4: Literary Taboos - Banned Books and Challenging Topics: Controversy in Children's and YA Literature Banned books, trauma, voice appropriation, #metoo, own voices - we'll be discussing the issues and taboos surrounding YA and Children's Book Publishing. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
Week 5: Teacher's Notes Exam Preparation Lecture about Middle Grade and YA Literature in the classroom and workshopping teacher's notes. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

Week 6: Heroes in Progress: Crafting Characters That Drive Youth Fiction Guest lecture by author Dr Richard Newsome. We'll also be discussing how dialogue can reveal character. Learning outcomes: L02, L03
Week 7: Teachers Notes Exam In-seminar, in-person exam. Learning outcomes: L01, L02

Week 8: The Architecture of Images & Silence - Building Lasting Mental Pictures Through Words & Building a Query Letter We'll discuss innovative writing techniques and we'll be workshopping how to write a query letter. Learning outcomes: L01, L03
Week 9: Breaking the Rules Brilliantly - Taking Form and Voice to the Next Level We'll discuss more innovative writing techniques and workshop your query letters/synopses Learning outcomes: L03
Week 10: King's Birthday - Writing Week No seminar this week. Use this week to continue rewriting your query letter and your opening scenes for final submission. Learning outcomes: L03

Week 11 - Macro to Micro: The Comprehensive Novel Editing Hierarchy. Guest lecture from UQP children's and YA publisher Cathy Vallance. We will also be workshopping peer's drafts too Learning outcomes: L02, L03

Week 12: The Art of Illustration The role of pictures in storytelling: a tour behind the scenes of the illustrated book, from picture book to graphic novel the history, power and profession of illustration. Come and meet our award winning guest author-illustrator, Kathleen Jennings. Learning outcomes: L01, L02
Week 13: VIVA Week Viva week by pre-scheduled appointment during the usual course seminar times of between 5pm and 7.30pm. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

policies_and_procedures: University policies and procedures apply to all aspects of student life. As a UQ student, you must comply with University-wide and program-specific requirements, including the: Student Code of Conduct Policy Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy and Procedure Assessment Procedure Examinations Procedure Reasonable Adjustments for Students Policy and Procedure Learn more about UQ policies on my.UQ and the Policy and Procedure Library . Course guidelines Communication Expectations While you are a student at UQ, all communication must be conducted according to the UQ Student Code of Conduct . The UQ Library has a helpful Communicate and collaborate online module . Email is the primary way for you to send messages to, and receive information from, the School and our staff. You must use your UQ email address (not a private address) to communicate with staff. You should add a clear subject line, including course code, and a 2-3 word statement. You can send email at any time, however, please do not expect responses outside normal working hours (Monday to Friday from ~8am to ~5pm). Emails that constitute bullying, harassment or discrimination against staff contravene the Student Code of Conduct. Emails like this will be reported to the University, and the matter will be pursued as misconduct. Learning activities
